title: Abigail Suggests Vampires Are No Longer Good Box Office Bets
text: Universal Pictures' horror-comedy Abigail opened to just $10.2 million, underperforming expectations and reinforcing a troubling trend: vampire movies have lost their commercial appeal. The film, directed by Radio Silence (the team behind Ready or Not), received positive critical reception and earned a solid B CinemaScore, yet audience interest remained tepid. With a modest $28 million budget, the studio may eventually break even, but a blockbuster hit this wasn't. This marks Universal's third vampire movie disappointment in a year. Both Renfield and The Last Voyage of the Demeter flopped in 2023, signaling deeper industry challenges. While Twilight generated $3.3 billion globally, establishing vampire narratives as commercially viable, that success proved exceptional rather than typical. Recent attempts have struggled: Morbius earned $167 million against a $75 million budget, while The Invitation (2022) managed only $33 million domestically. Even successful franchise entries like Underworld: Blood Wars (2017) achieved modest returns of $81 million. Independent productions such as What We Do in the Shadows found niche audiences, but mainstream vampire cinema remains challenged in the contemporary market. Director Robert Eggers' upcoming Nosferatu may test whether audiences are ready for a vampire revival, though industry observers remain cautious about the genre's viability.
